coredata.dc:title: Isomer and substituent engineering of TADF emitters toward tunable room-temperature phosphorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Quanyou
Rao, Junfeng
Wang, Huiyin
Ma, Jingyao
Yu, Fan
Zhang, Yunlong
Wang, Bingyang
Zhang, Jingrui
Cao, Hongtao
Wang, Hongjian
Peng, Xinzhe
Yang, Lei
Xie, Linghai
coredata.dc:description: Metal-free organic phosphorescent materials are crucial for the advancement of optoelectronics and bioelectronics. Nonetheless, achieving rational control over the performance of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) presents a significant challenge. In this study, two sets of isomeric thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) emitters with a donor–acceptor (D-A) configuration are designed and synthesized. Their RTP efficiency and lifetime are regulated by tuning their energy levels and molecular packing modes through isomer and substituent engineering. All of these materials exhibited afterglow emission, with the exception of 4-CzAIPh. A comprehensive investigation was conducted on the packing modes in the crystalline state, which revealed that the three RTP materials possess strong π-π interactions and numerous intermolecular forces, providing stability to the long-lived triplet excitons of RTP. Especially, 4-CzAIAd demonstrated the fastest intersystem crossing rate, the smallest fractional free volume and relatively larger spin–orbit coupling between its singlet and triplet states, as well as between its triplet state and ground state, which explains its longest phosphorescence lifetime of 301 ms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Isomerization
Substitution
D-A structures
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence
Room-temperature phosphorescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190090), the Project of State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays (GZR2022010020 and GDX2022010005), 1311 Talents Program of Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications (Dingxin), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22275098 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022 M711684), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (22KJB430036).

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190090), the Project of State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays (GZR2022010020 and GDX2022010005), 1311 Talents Program of Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications (Dingxin), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275098 and 62288102), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022 M711684), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (22KJB430036).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure and photographs of 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIPh and 4-CzAIPh. These photographs were taken at different time intervals before (in the first row) and after (in the second to fifth rows) excitation (365 nm) at 298 K.

Photophysical properties of 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIPh and 4-CzAIPh in crystalline state at 298 K. (a) PL spectra and phosphorescence spectra (delay 100 ms). (b) Time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves under nitrogen (excitation wavelength: 360 nm). (c) Time-resolved delayed fluorescence decay curves under nitrogen (excitation wavelength: 379 nm).
Crystal stacking and intermolecular interactions in (a) 3-CzAIAd, (b) 4-CzAIAd, (c) 3-CzAIPh and (d) 4-CzAIPh crystals.
Free volume area in the single crystal cells of (a) 4-CzAIAd, (b) 3-CzAIAd and (c) 3-CzAIPh. (d) Histogram of fractional free volume and lifetime.
The distribution of frontier molecular orbitals and energy levels of four kinds of molecular monomers and dimers.
The plots of the reduced density gradient (RDG) versus sign(λ 2)ρ for (a) 3-CzAIAd, (b) 4-CzAIAd, (c) 3-CzAIPh and (d) 4-CzAIPh.
Chemical structures and synthetic routes of 3-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIPh, 4-CzAIAd and 4-CzAIPh.
Summary of the photophysical properties of these four TADF emitters.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [a]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Measured in toluene solutions (10-5 M) at 298 K, [b] measured in crystalline state at 298 K, [c] E S1 and E T1 were estimated from the onset values of fluorescence spectra at 298 K and the low-temperature phosphorescence spectra at 77 K of the crystal films, [d] ΔE ST = E S1 − E T1.

head.ce:title: Isomer and substituent engineering of TADF emitters toward tunable room-temperature phosphorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays, Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biosensors, Institute of Advanced Materials, Centre for Molecular Systems and Organic Devices, Nanjing University of Posts & Telecommunications, 9 Wenyuan Road, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays
Centre for Molecular Systems and Organic Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays, Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biosensors, Institute of Advanced Materials, Centre for Molecular Systems and Organic Devices, Nanjing University of Posts & Telecommunications, 9 Wenyuan Road, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-free organic phosphorescent materials are crucial for the advancement of optoelectronics and bioelectronics. Nonetheless, achieving rational control over the performance of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) presents a significant challenge. In this study, two sets of isomeric thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) emitters with a donor–acceptor (D-A) configuration are designed and synthesized. Their RTP efficiency and lifetime are regulated by tuning their energy levels and molecular packing modes through isomer and substituent engineering. All of these materials exhibited afterglow emission, with the exception of 4-CzAIPh. A comprehensive investigation was conducted on the packing modes in the crystalline state, which revealed that the three RTP materials possess strong π-π interactions and numerous intermolecular forces, providing stability to the long-lived triplet excitons of RTP. Especially, 4-CzAIAd demonstrated the fastest intersystem crossing rate, the smallest fractional free volume and relatively larger spin–orbit coupling between its singlet and triplet states, as well as between its triplet state and ground state, which explains its longest phosphorescence lifetime of 301 ms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic light-emitting materials have recently undergone significant development and demonstrated great potential for application in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [1–4], information encryption [5–8], bio-imaging [9–14] and sensors [15–16]. Among them, organic room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials are notable for their ability to continue emitting light for a sustained period after excitation by an external light source, typically with a lifetime exceeding 0.1 s, and can be readily observed by the naked eye [17–23]. Organic chromophores absorb a photon with the appropriate energy, which results in electron excitation to a high singlet state (S n). Through either internal conversion or vibrational relaxation to the first singlet state (S 1), most excitons undergo radiative decay to the ground state (S 0) and emit short-lived fluorescence. However, as organic molecular orbitals are typically spin-forbidden, crossing from the singlet state to the triplet state is difficult. As a result, the majority of excitons in the triplet state undergo non-radiative transition to S 0 [24]. In order to realize RTP, two factors must be addressed: increasing the intersystem crossing (ISC) rate and suppressing non-radiative transitions of triplet excitons to S 0 [25]. Extensive research have been conducted to investigate strategies for achieving RTP, such as introduction of heteroatoms [26–28], formation of host–guest systems [29–35], strong coupling in H-aggregated near-planar molecules [36–38], and the use of metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [39–40].

ISC is a critical process for populating triplet excitons [41]. According to the El-Sayed’s rule [42], the incorporation of carbonyl or heteroatoms (N, S, and P) bearing lone pair electrons into organic emitters is known to generate n → π* transitions, thereby increasing the overlap between the singlet and triplet states, leading to a greater rate constant for ISC (k ISC), and facilitating the easier realization of RTP [43]. Besides, a small energy gap difference between the singlet state and the triplet state (ΔE ST) can also facilitate the ISC process [44–47]. Organic emitters possessing such a feature tend to exhibit thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) characteristics, which can be realized through the rational molecular design. Although strong π-π stacking may lead to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), it is noteworthy that aggregation often enables efficient RTP. For instance, in the case of H-aggregation, the stabilization of triplet energy and protection against quenching by oxygen and water are observed, resulting in diminished non-radiative transitions of triplet excitons and thereby enhancing the RTP properties [48–50]. Meanwhile, the dense π packing of TADF emitters could also lead to severe triplet–triplet annihilation (TTA) between the adjacent molecules [51–52], thus quenching the phosphorescence. Therefore, the electronic structures and aggregation structures are crucial for RTP properties of TADF emitters.

In this context, effective modulation of the exciton dynamics of the luminescent entity can be achieved by introducing substituents and altering their positions [36,53–55]. Li and coworkers [56] reported a series of phenothiazine derivatives and adjusted their RTP properties by modifying the type of substituents. It was found that the enhanced electron-accepting ability of the substituents could strengthen the π-π stacking interactions between molecules in the crystal, thereby stabilizing the excited triplet state and achieving ultra-long RTP. Chen et al. [36] synthesized two isomer emitters with RTP effect based on phthalimide (AI) and carbazole (Cz), which were also commonly used to design TADF molecules. RTP characteristics of these emitters were achieved in crystalline state through H-aggregation and adjustment of the position of n-orbital elements. However, achieving arbitrary properties over RTP performance poses a significant challenge. Herein, four isomeric carbazole-based TADF emitters, with AI as the acceptor (namely 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIPh and 4-CzAIPh), were designed and synthesized by varying the substituents on the nitrogen atom and the position of the carbazole donor. All of these materials, with the exception of 4-CzAIPh, exhibited afterglow emission, with lifetimes ranging from 167 to 301 ms. The effects of positional isomerization and substituents on the properties of RTP were systematically investigated in combination with theoretical calculations.

The chemicals and solvents, unless otherwise specified, were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China) or J&K Chemical Company (Beijing, China) without further purification. Toluene was dried over sodium benzophenone ketyl anion radical and distilled under a dry nitrogen atmosphere immediately prior to use. Other solvents were distilled under a dry nitrogen atmosphere immediately prior to use. 2-((3 s,5s,7s)-Adamantan-1-yl)-5-(9H-carbazol-9-yl) isoindoline-1,3-dione (4-CzAIAd) was synthesized according to our previous work [57]. The synthesis of the four target compounds was achieved using a two-step chemical transformation process starting from commercially available materials (Scheme 1 ). The 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on Bruker® AVIII 400 MHz NMR spectrometers. The single-crystal data collection was performed at 298 K or 100 K on a Bruker 2000 CCD area detector using graphite-monochromated Mo Kα radiation (λ = 0.71073 Å). All structures were solved by direct methods using SHELXS-2015 and refined against F2 using SHELXL-2015. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured with a Shimadzu UV-3150 spectrometer at 298 K, and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were processed on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) were determined using Hamamatsu Absolute PL Quantum Yield Spectrometer C11347. The PL transient decay curves were measured using a transient spectrometer (Edinburg FLS980) with a microsecond flash-lamp (uF900) were used; the uF900 flash lamp produces short, typically a few microseconds, and high irradiance optical pulses for phosphorescence measurements in the range from microseconds to seconds.

3-Chlorine-N-adamantyl phthalimide (3-ClAIAd). 3-Chlorophthalic anhydride (5.00 g, 27.47 mmol) and amantadine (6.00 g, 32.97 mmol) were added to a 50 mL reaction flask, and under nitrogen, acetic acid (15 mL), heated to reflux for 12 h. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, and a small amount of water was added, resulting in a white precipitate. The precipitate was filtered off with suction from the suspension, and the white powdery product, 3-chloro-adamantyl phthalimide (3-ClAIAd) (8.31 g, 96%) was obtained without further purification. Using dichloromethane (DCM) and petroleum ether (PE) (V PE: V DCM = 3:1) as the developing solvent for thin layer chromatography (TLC) analysis, the R f value of 3-ClAIAd is 0.62. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 7.70–7.64 (m, 1H), 7.59 (s, 1H), 7.58 (s, 1H), 2.51 (s, 6H), 2.17 (s, 3H), 1.82–1.67 (m, 6H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 168.32, 167.62, 135.62, 134.63, 134.39, 130.82, 127.28, 121.30, 61.13, 40.26, 36.30, 29.95.

3-Chlorine-N-phenyl phthalimide (3-ClAIPh). In a similar way to 3-ClAIAd, 3-ClAIPh was obtained as a white powder (6.50 g, 92%). Using a TLC analysis with a developing agent mixture of PE and DCM in a volume ratio of 3:1, we determined the R f value of 0.71 for 3-ClAIPh. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 7.90 (dd, J = 4.7, 3.6 Hz, 1H), 7.76–7.70 (m, 2H), 7.58–7.49 (m, 2H), 7.46 (s, 1H), 7.54–7.41 (m, 2H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 168.32, 167.62, 135.62, 134.63, 134.39, 121.30, 61.13, 40.26, 36.30, 29.95.

4-Bromo-N-phenyl phthalimide (4-BrAIPh). In a similar way to 3-ClAIAd, 4-BrAIPh was also obtained as a white powder (6.32 g, 94.7%). By using a certain proportion of DCM and PE (V PE: V DCM = 3:1) for TLC analysis, the R f value of 4-BrAIPh is 0.69. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 8.09 (d, J = 1.4 Hz, 1H), 7.93 (dd, J = 8.0, 1.6 Hz, 1H), 7.82 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 1H), 7.55–7.48 (m, 2H), 7.42 (dd, J = 7.5, 5.7 Hz, 3H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 166.53, 165.99, 137.52, 133.43, 131.44, 130.33, 129.40, 129.26, 128.39, 127.16, 126.54, 125.21.

3-Carbazolyl-N-adamantyl phthalimide (3-CzAIAd). 3-Chloro-adamantyl phthalimide (3-ClAIAd) (1.00 g, 3.17 mmol), carbazole (0.64 g, 4.76 mmol), cuprous iodide (CuI) (0.03 g, 0.16 mmol), K3PO4 (1.68 g, 7.93 mmol) and (1R,2R)-N,N'-dimethyl-1,2-cyclohexanediamine (0.45 g, 3.17 mmol) were dissolved in dry toluene (10.0 mL) and stirred at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, water was added to quench the reaction, the organic layer was extracted with DCM (3 × 50 mL), filtered and concentrated using a rotary evaporator. The residual solid was purified by flash column chromatography, R f = 0.42, using DCM and PE (V PE: V DCM = 3:1) as eluents to obtain 3-CzAlAd (0.57 g, 40%). 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 8.15 (d, J = 7.7 Hz, 2H), 7.91 (dd, J = 7.3, 1.2 Hz, 1H), 7.86 (t, J = 7.5 Hz, 1H), 7.78 (dd, J = 7.7, 1.2 Hz, 1H), 7.43–7.37 (m, 2H), 7.34–7.29 (m, 2H), 7.15 (d, J = 8.2 Hz, 2H), 2.43 (d, J = 2.6 Hz, 6H), 2.09 (s, 3H), 1.74–1.62 (m, 7H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 140.78, 135.22, 134.75, 134.35, 133.82, 126.04, 123.91, 122.19, 120.68, 110.06, 60.93, 60.93, 53.58, 40.19, 36.23, 29.89.

3-Carbazolyl-N-phenyl phthalimide (3-CzAIPh). Prepared according to the same procedure as used for 3-CzAIAd but using 3-ClAIPh. Separation and purification by silica gel column chromatography (V PE: V DCM = 3:1), R f = 0.49, 4-CzAlAd (0.44 g, 35%) was obtained as a white solid. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 8.15 (d, J = 7.6 Hz, 2H), 8.12 (d, J = 8.1 Hz, 2H), 8.01 (t, J = 7.6 Hz, 1H), 7.95 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 1H), 7.46–7.36 (m, 6H), 7.32 (q, J = 8.0, 7.5 Hz, 3H), 7.21 (d, J = 8.1 Hz, 2H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 166.59, 164.41, 140.72, 136.12, 135.12, 135.00, 134.33, 131.42, 129.10, 128.19, 126.62, 126.48, 126.18, 124.04, 123.28, 120.90, 120.71, 110.00.

4-Carbazolyl-N-phenyl phthalimide (4-CzAIPh). 4-bromo-phenyl phthalimide (4-BrAIPh) (1.00 g, 3.32 mmol), carbazole (0.83 g, 4.98 mmol), sodium tert-butoxide (t-BuONa, 0.38 g, 3.98 mmol), (t-Bu)3PHBF4 (0.05 g, 0.17 mmol) and palladium(II) acetate (0.05 g, 0.17 mmol) were dissolved in dry toluene (20 mL) and stirred at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, a small amount of water was added to quench the reaction, and the organic layer was extracted with DCM (3 × 50 mL), then dried over Na2SO4, filtered and concentrated using a rotary evaporator. The residue was purified by silica gel column chromatography (V PE: V DCM = 3:1), R f = 0.45, 4-CzAlAd (0.97 g, 75%) was obtained as a pale-yellow solid. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 8.22 (d, J = 1.7 Hz, 1H), 8.19 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 1H), 8.17 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 2H), 8.03 (dd, J = 7.9, 1.8 Hz, 1H), 7.56 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 2H), 7.52 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 4H), 7.49–7.43 (m, 3H), 7.37 (t, J = 7.4 Hz, 2H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 166.64, 166.61, 144.03, 140.02, 134.08, 132.04, 131.70, 129.42, 129.35, 128.43, 126.68, 126.64, 125.66, 124.26, 121.63, 121.37, 120.78, 109.65.

The chemical structures and purity of these target compounds were fully characterized by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, high resolution mass spectrometry (HR-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Fig. S6), and single crystal X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S7). The afterglow phenomenon of the four substances in crystalline state is shown in Fig. 1 . Interestingly, the luminescent compounds 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd, and 3-CzAIPh exhibit a persistent afterglow lasting up to 1.2 s, while their isomer 4-CzAIPh is not perceptible.

The photophysical properties of these molecules were studied in detail through UV–Vis absorption, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and phosphorescence spectrum in the dilute solution and crystalline state. The corresponding data are summarized in Table 1 . The absorption and PL spectra of these isomers in dilute toluene solutions (1 × 10-5 M) are shown in Fig. S1. The absorption spectra reveal conspicuous intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) peaks in the range of 350–400 nm for all four molecules. The PL spectra reveal maximum emission wavelengths ranging from 468 nm (blue) to 515 nm (blue-green). Fig. 2 a displays the PL spectra and phosphorescence spectra (delay 100 ms) of the four isomers in the crystalline state at 298 K. Intriguingly, apart from 4-CzAIPh, the other three isomers received a signal after the delay, consistent with the afterglow observed in Fig. 1. Isomers with substitutions on 3 and 4 sites of AI exhibit similar PL spectra, while those with RTP characteristics demonstrate a significant overlap with their own PL spectra. To comprehensively explore the characteristics of these compounds, additional investigations were conducted on their properties, including analyses of their time-resolved phosphorescence spectra (Fig. 2b) and time-resolved delayed fluorescence spectra in the crystalline state (Fig. 2c). Remarkably, prolonged delayed lifetimes were observed in these four compounds, with 4-CzAIAd demonstrating the longest phosphorescence lifetime of up to 301 ms. Their aggregation states were determined using XRD, which revealed discernible diffraction peaks in the 5°-30° range for the crystalline form, while the amorphous form showed negligible diffraction peaks (Fig. S6) [25,58]. According to the El-Sayed’s rule [42], the ISC rate is enhanced when the n → π* transition arises, and carbonyl can significantly accelerate the ISC process. The absorption spectra of these isomers provide direct evidence for this process, as shown in Fig. S4, where a strong n → π* transition is discernible.

Generally, the design principle of TADF molecules is based on the donor–acceptor (D-A) structure [59–61]. In present study, we employed the carbazole group as the electron donor and the phthalimide unit as the acceptor to construct the D-A structure. The ΔE ST were calculated based on the low-temperature phosphorescence at 77 K and fluorescence spectra at 298 K (Fig. S3) [62–63]. Furthermore, theoretical calculations and analyses of the single crystal structures of the four molecules reveal that the carbazole group predominantly hosts the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), while the phthalimide group is primarily responsible for the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) (Fig. S17-20). Notably, the HOMO and LUMO exhibit limited overlap, resulting in ΔE ST values of 0.09–0.31 eV, respectively. Such small ΔE ST values can enhance the ISC processes and strongly favor RTP. By altering phenyl and adamantyl substituents, the intermolecular stacking arrangement and torsion angle can be modified, resulting in differences in the luminescent properties of the molecules. The single crystal structure analysis demonstrated that the introduction of the adamantyl group led to a significant increase in the torsion angle between the donor and acceptor units, the dihedral angles between the carbazole group and phthalimide unit are 74.3°, 56.2°, 57.6° and 42.6° for 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIPh and 4-CzAIPh, respectively, which further increased the HOMO/LUMO separation. As such, we hypothesize that the electronic structure may not be the primary factor influencing room temperature phosphorescence in this system. However, the question persists: why does 4-CzAIPh, which has a similar electronic structure to the other compounds, fail to display room temperature phosphorescence? To address this, we analyzed the aggregation state of these single crystals.

To gain a deeper insight into how substitutions regulate the luminescence properties, single crystals of the four isomers can be readily obtained using the solvent diffusion method. Specifically, at 298 K, the single crystals of 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd, and 3-CzAIPh were successfully obtained from a solution of mixed dichloromethane (DCM) and n-hexane (HEX) with a volume ratio of 1:2. Similarly, the single crystals of 4-CzAIPh were obtained in the mixture of tetrahydrofuran (THF) and deionized water (DIW) with a volume ratio of 1:3. Single crystals of 3-CzAIAd and 3-CzAIPh belong to the monoclinic crystal system with the space groups of P21/n and P21/c, respectively, while the crystal structure of 4-CzAIAd adopts the triclinic style with the space group of P-1. The detailed data are summarized in Table S1. As shown in Fig. S12, unlike other crystals, 4-CzAIPh belongs to the monoclinic with Cc crystal system and has a dislocation parallel stacking and less crystal weak force. Each unit cell contains four molecules for 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIPh, and eight molecules for 4-CzAIPh. As shown in Fig. 3 , the molecules of 3-CzAIAd and 3-CzAIPh show a face-to-face stacking, with several interactions present in 3-CzAIAd (C-H···π 2.894 Å, 2.855 Å and 2.897 Å) and double π-π interactions between the carbazole and phthalimide group with a distance of 3.254 Å in 3-CzAIPh. Similarly, 4-CzAIAd also exhibits numerous intermolecular interactions in the single crystals (C-H···π 2.791 Å, C-H···O 2.498 Å). After a comparative analysis of the unit cells of the three single crystal unit cells, it was discovered that the quantity of free volume exhibits a strong correlation with the intensity of permanent phosphorescence [64–67]. Fig. 4 demonstrates that the cell’s fractional free volume for 4-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIAd, and 3-CzAIPh are 4.71%, 5.88%, and 7.49%, respectively. The ratio of 4-CzAIAd is smaller than that of 3-CzAIAd and 3-CzAIPh, which leads to a higher sustained phosphorescence lifetime in 4-CzAIAd.

To better understand the impact of intermolecular interactions on the persistent phosphorescence, time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations were conducted. Both isolated single molecules and coupled structures obtained from the single-crystal structures were adopted for the calculations. To consider the impact of ambient molecules in the crystals, quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics (QM/MM) calculations were also conducted. In the QM/MM approach, the excited-state structures and dynamics are calculated at the QM level, while the surrounding molecules are treated at the MM level [48,68].

The distribution of frontier molecular orbitals and energy levels of four types of molecular monomers and dimers were analyzed and presented in Fig. 5 . For isolated molecules, with the exception of 4-CzAIPh, only one triplet excited state (T 1) was located below S 1, whereas for dimers, two triplet excited states (T 1 and T 2) were observed below S 1. The adiabatic energy difference between S 1 and T 1 and between S 1 and T 2 are small enough to allow for the occurrence of the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process between them. This result suggests that two possible ISC processes (S 1 → T 1 and S 1 → T 2) can occur readily between S 1 and the low-lying triplet states for these molecules. Moreover, the dimer possessed more possible ISC transition channels, which promotes the production of strong phosphorescence with a prolonged lifetime [43,69]. According to the El-Sayed’s rule [42], ISC from 1ππ* to 3ππ* or 1nπ* to 3nπ* is prohibited due to the weak spin–orbit coupling (SOC). Indeed, the calculated SOC constants (ζ) for 3-CzAIPh and 4-CzAIAd are not very large. However, they exhibit a very small ΔE ST between S 1 and T 1, which promotes the ISC process. What’s more, we found that ΔE ST decreases when all monomers form a coupling structure with interaction. It is noteworthy that the ζ(T 1, S 0) values for 3-CzAIAd, 4-CzAIAd and 3-CzAIPh increase, while that for 4-CzAIPh decreases. According to the first-order perturbation theory and the Marcus semiclassical approach in the room-temperature regime, the rate constant (kISC ) of ISC is expressed as equation (1) [70–71],
(1)
k
ISC
∝
ξ
ST
2
exp
- Δ
E
ST
k B
T
where ζ ST and ΔE ST are the SOC constant (ζ) and energy gap between the involved singlet and triplet states, respectively, kB is the Boltzmann constant, and T is the temperature. Compared to the monomers, the coupling structures of 3-CzAIAd, 3-CzAIPh and 4-CzAIAd exhibit a reduction in ΔE ST and an increase in ζ(T 1, S 0), resulting in an increased kISC of the dimer. However, for 4-CzAIPh, although the ΔE ST of the dimer decreases, its ζ(T 1, S 0) also reduces, which can cause a decrease in the kISC . This observation is consistent with the aforementioned dynamics parameters of the excited states, which indicates that 4-CzAIPh exhibits a slow ISC process and thus does not possess the necessary conditions for efficient RTP.

To visualize and analyzed the non-covalent interactions between the dimers, reduced density gradient (RDG) plots (Fig. 6 ) and non-covalent interaction (NCI) isosurface plots (Fig. S21) were created. The NCI isosurface map clearly illustrates the location, strength and type of interactions between the individual molecules. Notably, the interaction area of 4-CzAIPh dimers is substantially larger than that of the other dimers (Fig. S22), and the RDG plots indicate fewer types of intermolecular forces compared to the other molecules. Hydrogen bonds can suppress the non-radiative decay of T 1 → S 0 through vibration decoupling, and triplet states with the characteristics of (π, π*) can attain bright and long life RTP. By observing the NCI surface map, it was found that 4-CzAIAd has more hydrogen bond interactions, which also indicates that its phosphorescence properties are more outstanding than others.

In summary, the RTP properties of the TADF emitters were regulated through isomerism and substituent engineering. The introduction of the adamantly group at the N-atom position of the phthalimide unit resulted in a conjugated breaking structure and led to a decrease in the ΔE ST values as compared to the phenyl group, which promoted the ISC processes. Furthermore, emitters with carbazole units substituted on the 4-position of the phthalimide group exhibited a more planar configuration and tighter crystal stacking structures, resulting in a smaller fractional free volume. In particular, the compound 4-CzAIAd demonstrated the fastest ISC rate, the smallest fractional free volume within the cell and its dimer exhibited relatively larger spin–orbit couplings between its S 1 and triplet states, as well as between its T 1 and S 0. When combined with the mutual interactions of the dimer, 4-CzAIAd exhibited a longest RTP lifetime of up to 301 ms. This work provides a promising strategy for constructing high-performance RTP materials with extended phosphorescence lifetime and elevated quantum yields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190090), the Project of State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays (GZR2022010020 and GDX2022010005), 1311 Talents Program of Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications (Dingxin), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275098 and 62288102), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022 M711684), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (22KJB430036).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144352.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
